questions.question: In what order do you use Activator to respond to streaming data?

options.A: Create actions, then connect data sources, then build rules
options.B: Connect to real-time data sources, create rules to check conditions, then execute actions when conditions are met
options.C: Build dashboards, then export data, then configure alerts
options.D: Define properties, then run notebooks, then connect data sources

questions.explanation: To use Activator you first connect to real-time data sources, then create rules to check for specific conditions, and when those conditions are met Activator executes actions such as sending alerts or running workflows.

questions.question: In the Activator business objects model, what does an 'object' represent?

options.B: An individual instance of a real-world thing you want to monitor, such as Package001 or Package002
options.C: A single column header in an eventstream
options.D: The action triggered when a rule fires

questions.explanation: Objects represent individual instances of the real-world things you want to monitor, like Package001, Package002, and Package003.

questions.question: What do 'events' contain in the Activator business objects model?

options.A: The rule definitions and action configurations
options.B: The actual data values that flow in from your data sources
options.C: The list of users authorized to view an object
options.D: The summarization window and step size settings

questions.explanation: Events contain the actual data values that flow in from your data sources. Activator uses this incoming data to automatically update property values for each object.

questions.question: Which summarization option is described as useful for detecting sensor failures by tracking how many readings you are getting?

options.B: Total
options.C: Count

questions.explanation: Count tracks how many readings you are getting, which is useful for detecting sensor failures.

questions.question: What does 'Step size' control in summarization?

options.A: How much historical data to include
options.B: How often to recalculate the value, for example every 5 minutes
options.C: The number of objects created per stream

questions.explanation: Step size controls how often to recalculate the value, for example every 5 minutes for near real-time monitoring.

questions.question: What do Fabric item actions execute?

options.B: Teams channel posts
options.C: Data pipelines or notebooks for further processing and analysis
options.D: SQL stored procedures in a warehouse

questions.explanation: Fabric item actions execute data pipelines or notebooks for other processing and analysis, used when you need to process more data or perform advanced analysis in response to conditions.

questions.question: Besides business objects, which alternative alerting approaches use the same Activator engine?

options.A: Dashboard alerts from Real-Time Dashboard visualizations, system event alerts, and query alerts from KQL Queryset results
options.B: Power BI subscriptions and report bookmarks only
options.C: Azure Monitor metric alerts and log analytics
options.D: Data warehouse maintenance jobs and statistics updates

questions.explanation: The Activator engine also supports creating dashboard alerts from Real-Time Dashboard visualizations, system event alerts to monitor Fabric workspace activities and OneLake file operations, and query alerts from KQL Queryset results and visualizations.

questions.question: A manufacturing plant wants maintenance teams alerted automatically when equipment temperatures exceed safe operating ranges. Which approach aligns with Activator's intended use?

options.A: Manually reviewing daily batch reports for high temperatures
options.B: Creating a rule that detects when temperature exceeds the safe range and triggers an alert action
options.C: Exporting telemetry to a CSV and emailing it weekly
options.D: Disabling sensors that report high values

questions.explanation: Activator is designed for scenarios where timely responses matter, such as automatically alerting maintenance teams when equipment temperatures exceed safe operating ranges through a rule that triggers an action.

questions.question: You are configuring Activator as a destination for an eventstream carrying package data with fields PackageId, Temperature, City, DeliveryState, and HoursInTransit. You want a separate object created for each package. Which field should be the unique identifier?

options.B: City
options.C: PackageId

questions.explanation: You choose the field that uniquely identifies the object. PackageId uniquely identifies each package, so it should be the unique identifier, letting Activator create separate package objects automatically.

questions.question: After choosing PackageId as the unique identifier, which fields would you most likely select as properties to monitor temperature problems and transit duration?

options.B: Temperature and HoursInTransit
options.C: DeliveryState and City

questions.explanation: To monitor temperature problems and how long packages are in transit, you would select Temperature and HoursInTransit as the properties to evaluate.

questions.question: As new events arrive in the eventstream feeding Activator, what happens to objects and properties?

options.A: Objects are deleted and recreated on every event
options.B: New events update existing object properties, new unique identifiers create new objects, and property values reflect the most recent data
options.C: Only the first event is ever processed; later events are ignored
options.D: Properties accumulate every historical value with no overwrite

questions.explanation: Once configured, new events update existing object properties, new unique identifiers automatically create new objects, and property values always reflect the most recent data from the stream.

questions.question: Package sensors produce noisy readings with brief spikes when packages are moved. You want to catch sustained temperature problems while ignoring brief fluctuations. What should you configure in the Monitor section?

options.B: Summarization using Average over a window, recalculated on a step interval
options.C: A Teams action with immediate delivery

questions.explanation: Summarization smooths out noise. Monitoring the Average temperature over a window (e.g., 10 minutes) updating on a step (e.g., every 5 minutes) catches sustained problems while ignoring brief fluctuations.

questions.question: You want Activator to fire only when a package's temperature stays above the safe limit for a sustained period rather than on every momentary spike. Which Occurrence setting should you choose?

options.B: When it has been true for a duration (for example, stays high for 15 minutes)
options.C: Once per object lifetime

questions.explanation: The 'When it has been true for' Occurrence option identifies persistent problems, such as a value staying high for 15 minutes, instead of alerting on every momentary reading.

questions.question: A team needs an immediate alert every single time a critical safety threshold is crossed, with no waiting period. Which Occurrence behavior fits?

options.A: When it has been true for 30 minutes
options.B: Every time, for immediate alerts
options.C: Range monitoring

questions.explanation: The 'Every time' Occurrence behavior is used for immediate alerts, firing each time the condition is met.

questions.question: You want to be notified when a temperature sensor stops reporting, indicating a possible sensor failure. Which detection approach in the Condition section should you use?

options.B: Change detection
options.C: Range monitoring
options.D: Missing data (No new events for more than a set duration)

questions.explanation: Missing data detection catches sensor failures, for example 'No new events for more than 30 minutes', signaling the sensor has stopped reporting.

questions.question: You want an alert when a package's temperature crosses a fixed safety limit such as greater than 68 degrees F. Which Condition detection approach should you use?

options.B: Missing data
options.C: Change detection

questions.explanation: Threshold monitoring alerts when values cross your safety limits, such as Temperature is greater than 68 degrees F.

questions.question: An analyst wants to be alerted when a metric rises above its normal baseline trend rather than a fixed number. Which Condition detection approach matches?

options.B: Change detection (monitor trends, such as temperature increases above baseline)
options.C: Missing data

questions.explanation: Change detection monitors trends, such as a temperature increasing above baseline, rather than against a fixed threshold value.

questions.question: You only want a temperature rule to evaluate packages classified as medicine. How do you restrict which events are evaluated?

options.A: Delete all non-medicine objects from the eventstream
options.B: Add a Property filter such as ColdChainType equals 'medicine'
options.C: Change the summarization to Count
options.D: Use a Power Automate action instead of a rule

questions.explanation: The Property filter section narrows which events get evaluated. A filter like ColdChainType equals 'medicine' limits the rule to cold chain medicine packages.

questions.question: When a rule fires, you need to automatically run a multi-step business process that updates a CRM, creates a ticket, and notifies a partner system. Which action type is designed for this?

options.B: Teams actions
options.C: Power Automate actions

questions.explanation: Power Automate actions execute multi-step business processes across different systems, automatically performing a series of tasks that would normally require manual steps across multiple applications.

questions.question: When a condition is detected, you want Activator to trigger heavier downstream data processing and advanced analysis on the incoming data. Which action type should you choose?

options.B: Fabric item actions that execute data pipelines or notebooks
options.C: Teams actions

questions.explanation: Fabric item actions execute data pipelines or notebooks for other processing and analysis, used when you need to process more data or perform advanced analysis in response to conditions.

questions.question: A data engineer already has KQL Queryset results visualizing anomalies and wants alerts without building a full business objects model. What can they do?

options.A: Nothing; Activator only supports business objects
options.B: Create query alerts from KQL Queryset results and visualizations using the same Activator engine
options.C: Convert the queryset into an eventstream destination first
options.D: Email the queryset manually on a schedule

questions.explanation: The Activator engine supports creating query alerts from KQL Queryset results and visualizations, an alternative approach that uses the same engine but interprets data differently than the business objects model.

questions.question: An operations lead wants to be alerted about Fabric workspace activities and OneLake file operations. Which Activator capability supports this?

options.B: System event alerts that monitor Fabric workspace activities and OneLake file operations
options.C: Property filters on package objects

questions.explanation: System event alerts monitor Fabric workspace activities and OneLake file operations, one of the alternative alerting approaches that use the Activator engine.

questions.question: A user wants alerts directly from a Real-Time Dashboard visualization without configuring eventstream objects. What is the correct approach?

options.A: Create dashboard alerts directly from the Real-Time Dashboard visualizations
options.B: Rebuild the dashboard as a Power BI report
options.C: Export the visualization to PDF and email it
options.D: Add a Property filter to the dashboard

questions.explanation: You can create dashboard alerts directly from Real-Time Dashboard visualizations, which use the same Activator engine as an alternative to the business objects model.

questions.question: Before Activator can evaluate conditions and trigger actions, what must you do regarding your data?

options.B: Configure how Activator should interpret your data for rule evaluation, for example via business objects
options.C: Disable summarization globally

questions.explanation: Before evaluating conditions and triggering actions, you must configure how Activator should interpret your data for rule evaluation, and one approach is using business objects.

questions.question: A supply chain manager wants to be notified when shipments deviate from planned routes or experience unexpected delays. How does this map to Activator concepts?

options.A: It cannot be done because Activator only handles temperature
options.B: Create rules on shipment properties (route/delay) that trigger notification actions when deviations occur
options.C: Use a semantic model to score deviations nightly
options.D: Schedule a pipeline to email a daily report

questions.explanation: Activator supports supply chain scenarios where managers are notified when shipments deviate from planned routes or experience delays, accomplished by rules on shipment properties that trigger actions.

questions.question: An IT operations team wants services automatically restarted when performance metrics indicate degradation. Which combination of Activator features supports this?

options.A: A rule detecting the degradation condition plus an action that runs an automated workflow or Fabric item
options.B: Only an email action with no rule
options.C: A Property filter with no Condition
options.D: A dashboard alert that cannot trigger any action

questions.explanation: IT operations teams can automatically restart services when performance metrics indicate degradation. This requires a rule that detects the condition and an action (such as a Power Automate flow) that performs the automated remediation.

questions.question: A financial institution wants unusual transaction patterns flagged for immediate review. Which Activator pattern fits best?

options.A: A rule detecting unusual patterns that triggers a Teams or email action for immediate review
options.B: Archiving transactions to OneLake without any rule
options.C: Disabling alerts to reduce noise
options.D: A static threshold that never changes regardless of data

questions.explanation: Financial institutions can flag unusual transaction patterns for immediate review. A rule detecting the pattern combined with a notification action delivers this immediate review capability.

questions.question: ColdChain Logistics ships temperature-sensitive medicines and streams sensor data (PackageId, Temperature, City, DeliveryState, HoursInTransit, ColdChainType) into a Fabric eventstream. They need: (1) a separate monitored entity per shipment, (2) alerts only on medicine shipments, (3) tolerance for brief temperature spikes when packages are handled, (4) escalation only when temperature stays unsafe for a sustained period, (5) immediate team coordination when triggered, and (6) automatic launch of a downstream notebook to recompute spoilage risk. Which end-to-end configuration satisfies ALL requirements?

options.A: Use PackageId as the unique identifier to create per-package objects; add a Property filter ColdChainType equals 'medicine'; in Monitor use Average summarization over a window with a step; in Condition use Threshold monitoring with Occurrence 'When it has been true for' a sustained duration; configure a Teams action plus a Fabric item action that runs the notebook
options.B: Use Temperature as the unique identifier; no Property filter; Count summarization; Occurrence 'Every time'; only an email action
options.C: Create no objects; rely solely on a dashboard alert; use Change detection with no filter; trigger only a Power Automate flow
options.D: Use City as the unique identifier; filter on DeliveryState; Minimum summarization; Missing data condition; only a Fabric item action

questions.explanation: PackageId as the unique identifier creates a separate object per shipment (req 1). A Property filter ColdChainType equals 'medicine' restricts evaluation to medicine shipments (req 2). Average summarization over a window with a step smooths out brief spikes (req 3). Threshold monitoring with the 'When it has been true for' occurrence escalates only on sustained unsafe temperature (req 4). A Teams action enables immediate team coordination (req 5), and a Fabric item action runs the notebook for downstream spoilage analysis (req 6). Only option A satisfies every requirement.

questions.question: You want a missing-data rule that fires when a sensor has not reported for over 30 minutes. Which condition expression represents this?

options.B: No new events for more than 30 minutes
options.C: Average over 30 minutes is less than baseline

questions.explanation: Missing data detection catches sensor failures with a condition such as 'No new events for more than 30 minutes', firing when no events arrive within the specified duration.

questions.question: A medicine cold-chain rule should evaluate only events where ColdChainType is medicine AND City is Seattle. Using the Property filter capability, which configuration is valid?

options.A: Combine two filters: ColdChainType equals 'medicine' and City equals 'Seattle'
options.B: Combine four filters because only four are allowed
options.C: Use a single filter that concatenates both fields into one string
options.D: Filters cannot reference City, only Temperature

questions.explanation: The Property filter section lets you combine up to three filters. Combining ColdChainType equals 'medicine' with City equals 'Seattle' is a valid two-filter configuration for precise targeting.

questions.question: You configure Monitor to track the Average of Temperature over a 10-minute window recalculated every 5 minutes. Which pair of settings did you set?

options.A: Window size = 5 minutes, Step size = 10 minutes
options.B: Window size = 10 minutes, Step size = 5 minutes
options.C: Window size = 0, Step size = 0
options.D: Window size and Step size are not used with Average

questions.explanation: Window size is how much historical data to include (10 minutes) and Step size is how often to recalculate (every 5 minutes). The example monitors the average over 10-minute windows updating every 5 minutes.

questions.question: To detect possible sensor failures by counting how many readings arrive over a period, which summarization aggregation should the Monitor attribute use?

options.B: Total
options.C: Count

questions.explanation: Count tracks how many readings you are getting and is specifically called out as useful for detecting sensor failures.

questions.question: A rule must trigger only when the summarized unsafe temperature persists for 15 minutes rather than on each evaluation. Which Occurrence setting encodes that logic?

options.B: When it has been true for 15 minutes
options.C: No new events for more than 15 minutes

questions.explanation: The 'When it has been true for' occurrence identifies persistent problems, such as a condition staying true (high) for 15 minutes, instead of firing every time.

questions.question: You need a rule that captures the extreme high temperature value within each evaluation window, since extremes matter most for spoilage. Which summarization should you select?

options.B: Maximum
options.C: Count

questions.explanation: Minimum/Maximum summarization catches the extreme values that matter most. To capture the highest temperature in each window, select Maximum.

questions.question: Given the eventstream fields PackageId, Temperature, City, DeliveryState, HoursInTransit, you want one object per shipment with Temperature and HoursInTransit monitored. Which setup correctly maps unique identifier and properties?

options.A: Unique identifier = PackageId; properties = Temperature, HoursInTransit
options.B: Unique identifier = Temperature; properties = PackageId, City
options.C: Unique identifier = City; properties = DeliveryState only

questions.explanation: PackageId uniquely identifies each shipment, creating one object per package, and Temperature plus HoursInTransit are selected as the properties to monitor, exactly matching the documented example.
